speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number three. The survivor being interviewed is Hilde Scheraga.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Scheraga, you were wearing a, a star. And yet all these-- and these visitors--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I never wore a star.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, you didn't the--

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, I got the star. But I-- no. First of all, you only had to wear it when we-- when you went out.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't go out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Since I didn't go anywhere-- I didn't go out-- I didn't have to wear it. I, I think I wore it, you know, very, very few times. The children did not seem to mind. They didn't know what a Jew was. They had never met one, probably. And to them, it meant nothing. There had not been any anti-Semitism in Belgium. And Belgium was a very liberal country. And that-- they hated the Germans, partly because of what happened during the first world war.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so my problem was [LAUGHS] fighting them, because I, you know, I didn't want to be German. I couldn't help being German. But I know it's certainly something that I, I could have, I could have handled being accused of being a Jew. I had experience dealing with that, but not having experience dealing with what they used to call me, [NON-ENGLISH]. [NON-ENGLISH] is a derogatory word for a German. It's as if you call a, a Jew a Yid, you know? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So-- but, but I never understood. And I've discussed it with other Jewish children who now live in the United States that I'm in touch with who were with me in the orphanage, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] why was it that we were never bothered? The Germans must've known. I mean, they never even came to look, as far as I know. I mean, it was a large place. I did hear that they went to one place, one of the buildings but did not-- but why didn't they look into all of them? You know, why didn't they investigate more carefully? They could have.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we think that the director of the orphanage, the-- who was a, a nobleman, he was called the [NON-ENGLISH]. Anyone with the name [NON-ENGLISH] in front of their name was, you know, an aristocrat of some kind. And we think that he had some-- you know, that he had made some kind of a deal with the Germans. But it's pure guesswork. All we know is that-- we didn't know during the war, of course-- that we were very fortunate insofar as no one ever came to look for us, even though we stopped going out. And we stopped going to school. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you know, growing up in a more normal environment. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I continued to be very, very fearful of being discovered. And I felt good that I wasn't-- that I didn't look Jewish. I thought that that might help.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said you hated being German. What did you think about being Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that didn't bother me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there pride? Or was there anger? Or was there--

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a bit of both. No, at that time, no, at that, I, I liked being Jewish. I was very proud of it. I liked-- you know, I was different. And I was proud of it. And it gave me strength. As I said, I became my own person. I felt I didn't need anybody. And I thought that that was a very positive thing. And being Jewish certainly didn't bother me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] many kinds of what you would call today, normal activities, dating or romances or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Nothing like that?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, that was strictly verboten, strictly forbidden. There were a few-- the age range, I guess, was from two through 18. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Most kids, of course, fell in the middle. I was aware of some dating after liberation with the American soldiers. And that was quickly put to bed when these kids were found out. Personally, I was, at 16, probably looked like an 11-year-old. So it's, it never, it was never a problem with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about the day of liberation. You said you remember it so vividly.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. Yeah. Well, we knew that the English were coming, that the English liberated Brussels. And I can only describe it as a tidal wave of we all-- all the children, all those who could walk, we all left. We all-- the children and, and the adults, I think, everybody just went downtown to see the soldiers. It was, you know, an exhilarating experience. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they came in on their tanks. I, I, I only remember the tanks, you know, coming in through the main street, and the soldiers throwing candies and chewing gum, which I had never seen-- chocolate bars, oranges, I mean, things that we hadn't seen in years. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So this-- it, it was magic. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was sheer magic. For me, of course, it was a great relief. I no-- [CRYING] I no longer had fear, you know, of being discovered and, and endangering the, the entire orphanage. [DRINKS] And we-- on, on the way downtown, which may have been a-- it was quite a long walk. But we ran. It, it was just-- there was no holding anyone back. We saw many discarded German uniforms on the street, which was rather interesting. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We saw, I guess what used to be German buildings, being, being raided, which perhaps, was not so nice, but was understandable. You know, everything was taken out, because the shortages in all of Europe, including Belgium, were absolutely horrendous. I mean, for about two years, I think, we wore wooden shoes. Because there were no shoes. You know, and children's feet grow. And, and there were no shoes. Wooden shoes were plentiful. I think somebody must have made them. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And on an aside, you know, now we go to Holland. And we admire the wooden shoes. And we buy them. They're very uncomfortable, especially when you don't have socks to put in them. There was no-- there was-- the clothing was a problem, although we always had something to wear. But shoes, we didn't have. But that's an aside.

speaker: interviewee
text: So you know, the, all the German occupied buildings, houses, whatnot, were immediately raided. And everything was stolen. But you know, I applauded it, personally, although a lot of people, you know, frowned on that. I mean, it was a very Catholic society with, you know, very strict rules. But it was a tremendous-- for me-- a tremendous feeling of liberation. And how, how long did that celebration go on?

speaker: interviewee
text: It-- well, we only-- we were, you know, that, that was the only time we were allowed to go out to that. You know, as I said, I don't think they could have stopped us.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay in the orphanage?

speaker: interviewee
text: We stayed for a few hours, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the orphanage, I mean, after the liberation.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, after liberation, I stayed until May of-- March of '46. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I had-- I didn't know, of course, if my mother was alive. I didn't know what would happen to me. Of course, I, I did go back to school as soon as the war was over, which was wonderful. But I had now-- I was now three years behind my contemporaries. I had lost the one year when I moved from Germany to Belgium. And then I lost two years when I didn't go to school at all in Belgium. And I was now three years behind. And that bothered me a great deal. So I maneuvered to skip a few grades. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] but I was still behind. I couldn't skip three grades. I think I skipped one, and then later on, another one. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was very anxious to be educated. I loved reading, but I had very little material to read. It's strange. That reading-- we weren't even allowed to read. I mean, you know, everything-- every, every moment of your day was pretty well structured. And I remember, you know, hiding with the rare occasions when I had a book, to-- hiding to read it. I remember reading Dumas' Somehow, I got a hold of that series and a, a few other books, but very, very little.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was very anxious to catch up with my education. I dreamed of coming to the United States. So I thought I should learn English. So I managed to go to a school where English was taught as, as a second language. Didn't do me much good, [LAUGHS] but I was-- you know, I took a few classes in English. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Because I didn't know what the future held. And by now, I was 14, 15-years-old, so it became, again, a problem. What, what would happen to me? Was I going to be stuck in this orphanage for the rest of my life? You know, what would I do when I turned 18, which was the age when you left this place? You were usually placed somewhere, either as an apprentice to, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] say, to a dress maker or in one of the, you know, manual fields. And I was trying to figure out what would I do? I didn't like to sew. And [LAUGHS] so I mean, all these things were certainly going through my mind. And of course I always wondered what happened to my mother, would I hear from here again? And was there word from your father of his trying to, to get you an, an affidavit, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I think so. Yeah. Yeah. Mhm. But again, this would take time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there Jewish organizations that came to see you, and--

speaker: interviewee
text: After the war? No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. No. I think later on in, in 1945, the refugees started streaming in. And Jewish organizations were very involved in helping the refugees, the concentration camp survivors.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were really left in limbo for--

speaker: interviewee
text: For a while, yeah. Yeah. I inquired about my brother. And I found out where he was. And I wrote to him. And I got back an answer from the family that he was living with to tell me that he was there and that he was fine. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was in touch with my father and with my brother, very-- you know, my brother very little. My father sent me some clothes, which was very nice. I think I was still wearing the same clothes I came to Belgium with when I was eight. You know, they had been stretched and altered and patched up. But [LAUGHS] I badly needed clothes.

speaker: interviewee
text: There just was nothing. There was nothing available in Belgium during the war, not anything. There were no fabrics, no, no clothes to speak of, especially not for, for, you know, for a place like an orphanage, where other things were more important-- to feed us, to keep us warm if possible. And that reminds me. One winter, we had no heat. And we spent a great deal of time in bed. And when the weather became warmer, all the pipes had frozen, the water pipes. And they burst. And we were busy for hours, mopping up that ice cold water. I'll never forget it. We had to do it. And the water was so cold. And the-- it, and it took a while to plug up the pipes to stop the water from coming down. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So that's-- you know, I think it's sometimes hard to visualize what did these-- these things that we take for granted, like heat and food. It may not be good food, but at least it's food, you know? If you have enough food, if you have bread, if you have potatoes, that's wonderful.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were very, very hungry much of the time. And we did used to go sneak out in the fields and steal some potatoes. This orphanage was located in what was then a rural area. It's now highly developed. And occasionally, we. we would find an apple or potato. And we'd eat the potatoes raw, the apples raw. Beet roots, we used to eat raw. Every, every-- because we weren't allowed to do it. But occasionally, we did sneak out and get away with it. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the discipline strict?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was very strict, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it harsh?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was not harsh. But you could be, as punishment, be deprived of food, which there was little enough. So you made pretty sure that you wouldn't miss a meal.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you hear from your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. I didn't hear from my mother. One day in, in June of 1945, she, she came. [CRYING] She just appeared one day. It was late June of 1945. I think it was June 25th, something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Bless you.

speaker: interviewee
text: She had been liberated in January of 1945 by the Russians as they were marching from Stutthof to Berlin. They were being marched. And she said she couldn't, she couldn't have survived much longer than she did on this march. And one day, they were just-- the SS disappeared. And they were left on their own.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you called into the office? Or how-- how did you meet her?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. That morning-- once, you know, once in a while, something-- we had something special, a special treat. That morning, we went swimming, which was very unusual. We, you know-- like here, there are places that invite. You may get a call. You know, we're bringing the handicaps in to see a show. Or we're, you know, we're putting up a, a program in one of the high schools in which you buy a ticket for the handicaps. And this reminds me very much, because this happened to us. At Christmas, some institution somewhere, some organization, might be putting up a show. And they'd invite all the orphanages or something like that. So on this particular Sunday morning, we'd gone swimming. And while I was away, my mother came. And so when I came back, the kids who realized who she was, even though she didn't speak a word of French, came running. And they just sort of jumped and ran around me. They didn't tell me anything. And you now, there was my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there she was.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I mean, I hadn't seen her in seven years. And she looked so different. And she-- you know, I was certainly very, very different too. I was eight. I was now 15. That's a big, big age difference. Even though I, you know, I was scrawny looking, still, I was not a little child anymore. I'd forgotten every word of German. And she, of course, didn't speak any French. So communicating was a problem. She came. And her-- all her possessions was the dress she was wearing. And she was wearing-- a bag, handbag she was carrying. That's all she owned. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Now how long was this after she was-- after liberation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Liberation? It was from January to June, six months. It took her six months to make her way to Belgium. It was-- there was no one helping. There was very little help available on the road to Belgium, as it were. Eventually, she met up with the Americans. And they helped her. They flew her to Belgium. She was going to walk. And I mean, she had no money. She had nothing. She had no documents. So it was very difficult to travel without documents. It's-- in Europe, it's always a problem, even today. You know, if you live in any European countries, you carry an identity card. And you know, you need to show it. In those days, it was even worse.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was very difficult for her to travel. And yet, traveling is what she had to do. There were no trains. There were no buses. I mean, She had to hitch rides. Occasionally, there was a train. And she had no money.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I think the-- this is how she tells the story, anyhow. She told a story that everyone was so afraid of the survivors that they let them ride the trains without money. Or they let them-- they gave them food if they asked. You know, my mother would knock on doors and ask for food. And, and they would give her food. And she'd ask to be allowed to sleep. And they'd allow her to sleep in the barn. Occasionally, she'd ask me to-- if she could wash herself. So they'd give her a basin of cold water. And she'd wash herself-- wash her clothes, too. And put them on wet, because there was-- she had no, she had nothing else to wear except the clothes she was wearing. So it was a, it was a very difficult journey, to put it mildly, especially after having survived the concentration camps and, you know, the, the, the more than two years, close to 2 and a half years, being incarcerated. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So anyway, I-- the orphanage looked up for me the names of some Jewish organizations. They found out. She stayed with me overnight. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: They gave us a room. I mean, they fixed up a room with two beds. And they were very kind and made some inquiries. And I took her then to this organization, which gave her some money. And she was able to find a little room in a loft somewhere. And they gave her a little money for food, but not really a lot. But there were all kinds of places that fed the refugees. I guess you would call them soup kitchens today. So she used to go to the soup kitchens and eat. And then I remember going with her to pick up some clothes. There were these places with clothes all-- you know, with just masses of clothing all mixed up, I mean, shoes all mixed up. But you know, this was nothing. This was-- didn't faze my mother at all. It bothered me a lot. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It bothered me, not, not because [LAUGHS] it was difficult to find the clothes that fit her. But it bothered me to see all these people. And there was-- I, I recall hundreds of them. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Looked so sad, and looked so emaciated, and who looked sickly, and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] who, who behaved in, in strange ways that I didn't really want to be involved with. And at long last, I had somewhere to go on the one Sunday a month when we were allowed to go out. And the first few months, my mother didn't have any money. So she used to take me to these soup kitchens or you know, take me with her to-- she'd take me with her to wherever she was going. And she felt very much at home with the survivors. Those were her friends. But I finally said, this is not where I want to be. This is not a reality that I want to handle, certainly not at that point in my life. You know, I was dreaming of other things. And for better or worse, I, I didn't want to know about these things. So she stopped taking me there. And she decided she had to make more money. My father did send some money. But in those days, it, it took time. It didn't-- it didn't happen overnight. And so she got a job as a, as a cook and housekeeper with a family. And she used to sneak a little food out for me and-- so that we'd. you know-- she could give me a meal. I mean, I couldn't spend the day with her and not eat.

speaker: interviewee
text: So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] eventually, I guess, my German came back. Or a little bit of it came back. It took, took a while, though. It, it didn't happen overnight. And just reconnecting with your mother, it's not like picking up the pieces that you, you know, put aside yesterday. It's not like going away to a summer camp or to college and being away from home for a month, or a year, or two years. I mean, this was seven years during my growing years, and in very abnormal, unusual times.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we'd both had very different experiences. And we really couldn't dialogue. We were not equipped. We didn't have the-- I wouldn't even call it vocabulary. I don't think we had the mental ability to, to truly communicate. Her-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] her joy was to see that I had survived. And that was enough. I don't think she was interested in anything else. I had survived. I was in good health. I, you know, I was growing up. And the orphanage, to her, was beautiful, which it was. You know, it was intact. It hadn't been bombed. It hadn't been damaged. And that was-- that's really all she cared about. I mean, this is what she had dreamed about during her concentration camp experience, that we would be left-- that we would-- her children would survive. She knew that my father would survive since was the-- or she assumed that he would survive since he was in the United States. In the United States, you know, no one could do any wrong in, in our minds then. It was Heaven and Superman combined. It was a Heaven full of super people who could do no wrong and who had no problems, who lived happily ever after. And, and my father was part of that society. So she was satisfied that, you know, her world was coming back, which it was. But it, it didn't happen overnight. I don't know that it ever happened. But it was certainly a, a very difficult process, reconnecting with a mother that you hadn't seen in, in so many years and, you know, communicated with very minimal. I mean, I forgot to tell you the mail was always censored. So it was very little one could write. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] It's just incredible that ordinary letters, you know, were censored. But they were. It was very obvious. They were always opened.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your plans were to get out of there as soon as possible?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, they were. But I-- of course, my mother could not-- I could not move out. Because my mother had no means of keeping me. She had this tiny room [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] with, you know, I think, a little burner and sink or something. I don't-- didn't have a bathroom. I think she used the toilet of the family that rented her the-- this room. And in fact, a year ago, I went back to see that house. And it had been totally renovated. So I-- you know, it was-- I had never been back there before. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] but, you know, to her, these things didn't pose any problems. And anything was better than where she came from. So-- and my mother was a very upbeat sort of person. I was far more introspective and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] moody.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know what she had experienced?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, I didn't know. And I don't think I had the words. We, we didn't have the vocabulary at first. We couldn't-- you know, as I said, I didn't speak German. I no longer spoke German. I hated German. To this day, I can't stand hearing German. And I will not speak it if I can get away with it. She, of course, this was her language. It was the only language she spoke. And she didn't have, you know, this feeling towards the language, which is stupid. But [LAUGHS] I always shudder when I hear German. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] so I really didn't want to learn German again. But of course, I had to. Because I knew I had to talk with my mother and eventually with my father, since once I came to America, I, I didn't know English. Certainly, the little bit I was learning at school wasn't doing me that much good. And I realized that I had to learn German again.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did it take before you were able to leave?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. Before-- it took us another year. We left in, in March, early March of 1946. My mother came back in June of '45. So it wasn't that long. We also went to see my brother. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I was allowed to accompany her. They were very-- the home-- the orphanage was very kind. You know, they knew that this was an unusual situation. And I was now no longer a little child. I was 15. And I was certainly capable of traveling with an adult. So they allowed me to go with her to see him. I did feel as if I was really taking care of her rather than the other way around, since she didn't know-- you know, she was not familiar with French, with the language and with the money. And-- and was just-- you know, I, I, I knew how to find my way. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I was, you know, very proud about that. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My father, I knew, was sending us papers. Because we had to travel to Antwerp many times. And we had to take my brother along. And this became a problem.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because of his behavior?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, because of his behavior. In fact, we had to take him to the American [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] consulate in Antwerp.
